coredata.dc:title: Red-emitting carbon dots for fabrication of high-quality white LEDs with a color rendering index of 97
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Fanghao
Wang, Kexin
Guan, Ruifang
Zhang, Hao
coredata.dc:description: Red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs), a novel class of environmentally benign, non-toxic, and pollution-free nanomaterials, have garnered considerable attention in the field of solid-state white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) owing to their exceptional optical properties. Herein, we synthesized R-CDs-1 with a 671 nm emission using a simple solvent thermal method employing pyromellitic acid and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene as precursors. Structural characterization and density functional theory (DFT) simulations were employed to demonstrate that the reduction in the bandgap due to structural changes leads to a redshift in the emission spectrum of R-CDs-1. By blending R-CDs-1 with commercial phosphors, high-quality WLEDs were successfully fabricated, achieving a high color rendering index (CRI) of 97, approaching the highest reported values to date. Compared to other works, the prepared WLED exhibited a color temperature closer to natural light, offering superior performance for practical illumination. This work significantly contributes to elucidating the red emission mechanism of carbon dots and advances the application of R-CDs in the development of environmentally friendly, non-toxic, high-quality white light illumination devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red-emitting
DFT
WLED
High CRI

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Characterization of fluorescent properties of R-CDs
The effect of R-CDs structure on fluorescence performance
Solid-state luminescence and WLEDs based on R-CDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a–e) UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5 and R-CDs-0.25. Red lines represent absorption spectra, blue lines represent excitation spectra, and black lines represent emission spectra; (a1-e1) Emission spectra, (a2-e2) Fluorescence lifetime spectra and (a3-e3) Tauc plots of R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5 and R-CDs-0.25; and their corresponding transitions. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a–e) TEM images and (a1-e1) Size distribution of R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5 and R-CDs-0.25; (a2-e2) HR-TEM images of R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5, and R-CDs-0.25, with insets showing the lattice spacing (marked with red circles), and (a3-e4) corresponding simulated size images. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a–e) XPS spectra of R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5 and R-CDs-0.25 (Illustration: percentage of elements in R-CDs); and high-resolution XPS (a1-e1) C 1s spectrum, (a2-e2) N 1s spectrum, and (a3-e3) O 1s spectrum of R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5 and R-CDs-0.25.

DFT calculations of the five sample models. (a) Molecular structures of the five models. (b) Calculated HOMO and LUMO of the models R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5, and R-CDs-0.25. HOMO: Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital, LUMO: Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital. (c) Fluorescence spectra of R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5, and R-CDs-0.25 (from left to right).

(a) CIE coordinates of WLEDs prepared using R-CDs; (b) Illumination effect of WLEDs in a dark environment; (c) Corresponding electroluminescence spectra (inset: actual photos under powered and unpowered conditions); (d) Colored strip photos under WLED illumination; and (e) Colored strip photos under sunlight illumination.
Schematic diagram illustrating the synthesis of R-CDs-1.
Fluorescence emission, lifetime, and QY of R-CDs.
Comparison of performance parameters of WLEDs fabricated using red-emissive carbon dots.

head.ce:title: Red-emitting carbon dots for fabrication of high-quality white LEDs with a color rendering index of 97

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan, Shandong 250022, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study reports a simple solvothermal method for synthesizing red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs-1) with a peak emission at 671 nm, using anhydrous ethanol as the solvent and pyromellitic acid and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene as precursors. Structural characterization and density functional theory (DFT) simulations demonstrate that the reduction in the bandgap, resulting from changes in the conjugated structure and amine content of the carbon dots, is the primary cause of the redshift in the emission spectrum. By mixing R-CDs-1 with commercial phosphor powders, high-quality white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) were successfully fabricated, achieving a color rendering index (CRI) of 97, close to the highest value reported to date. The fabricated WLEDs exhibit a color temperature closer to natural light, offering excellent performance for practical lighting applications.

Red-emitting carbon dots (R-CDs), a novel class of environmentally benign, non-toxic, and pollution-free nanomaterials, have garnered considerable attention in the field of solid-state white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) owing to their exceptional optical properties. Herein, we synthesized R-CDs-1 with a 671 nm emission using a simple solvent thermal method employing pyromellitic acid and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene as precursors. Structural characterization and density functional theory (DFT) simulations were employed to demonstrate that the reduction in the bandgap due to structural changes leads to a redshift in the emission spectrum of R-CDs-1. By blending R-CDs-1 with commercial phosphors, high-quality WLEDs were successfully fabricated, achieving a high color rendering index (CRI) of 97, approaching the highest reported values to date. Compared to other works, the prepared WLED exhibited a color temperature closer to natural light, offering superior performance for practical illumination. This work significantly contributes to elucidating the red emission mechanism of carbon dots and advances the application of R-CDs in the development of environmentally friendly, non-toxic, high-quality white light illumination devices.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Compared to traditional light sources, WLEDs offer advantages such as energy efficiency, environmental friendliness, long lifespan, compact size, and reliability [1], making them the most promising solid-state lighting devices of the 21st century. Commercial WLED lighting typically employs phosphor conversion technology. Therefore, the improvement in LED device performance relies on the development of high-quality phosphors [2]. Commercial WLEDs are typically categorized into two types. The first type achieves white light emission by applying a yellow-emitting phosphor coating onto blue LED chips [3]. Due to the lack of red emission, prepared WLEDs often exhibit a CRI below 75. The second type involves the application of a mixture of red, green, and blue phosphors on ultraviolet (UV) chips as a light conversion layer, resulting in WLEDs with significantly higher CRI values. The CRI is a crucial performance metric for WLEDs, as accurate color perception is essential in professional fields such as medicine, art, and design, where a CRI exceeding 90 is typically required. Therefore, the development of high-performance phosphor materials is vital to meet elevated lighting standards. Furthermore, traditional WLEDs often utilize phosphors containing rare-earth elements or heavy metal ions, which pose several common issues: high synthesis temperatures, high costs, non-renewability, and significant environmental pollution [4]. With increasing public concern over resources, environment, and health, the search for new, non-toxic, efficient, and inexpensive WLED phosphors has become urgent.

In this context, carbon dots have emerged as a promising new luminescent material due to their low toxicity [5], tunable fluorescence emission [6], low cost [7], and excellent photostability [8]. They are gaining considerable attention as potential non-toxic, efficient, and cost-effective alternatives for WLED manufacturing. R-CDs can address the aforementioned deficiencies by providing red emission, making them ideal candidates for WLEDs. In recent years, numerous researchers have reported successful fabrication of WLEDs using R-CDs. For example, Kang et al. synthesized red-emitting carbon dots at 600 nm via hydrothermal methods using o-phenylenediamine, achieving WLEDs with coordinates (0.32, 0.33), correlated color temperature (CCT) of 6067 K, and a CRI of 78.2 [9]. Xu et al. synthesized o-CDs emitting at 620 nm through solvent thermal reaction, achieving WLEDs with coordinates (0.34, 0.34) and a CRI of 86.9 [10]. Zhai et al. employed microwave-assisted heating to produce r-CDs emitting at 650 nm, resulting in WLEDs with CIE coordinates (0.33, 0.33) and CRI of 92 [11]. Despite these achievements in carbon dot-based LED materials, the introduction of shorter wavelengths of light may lead to lower color rendering indices, thereby still restricting their application in indoor lighting. Furthermore, most R-CDs require complex purification methods and often exhibit weaker emission properties, resulting in poor performance in practical applications. Therefore, proposing a straightforward one-step synthesis strategy for high-quality R-CDs and applying them to prepare WLEDs with superior color rendering quality holds significant importance.

This paper describes the preparation of near-infrared emitting carbon dots, denoted as R-CDs-1, using a simple solvothermal method, which exhibit emission at 5 nm. Using techniques such as transmission electron microscopy and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy, the structural changes were investigated, along with DFT simulations to explore the influence of carbon dot structures on fluorescence. In R-CDs-1, photoluminescence is predominantly influenced by the carbon core state. The increase in amide content and conjugated structures contributes to the redshift in the emission spectrum. Structural characterization and theoretical calculations together demonstrate that the reduction in the bandgap caused by structural changes facilitates the fluorescence redshift. R-CDs-1 demonstrate excitation-independent behavior and excellent fluorescence stability. By blending R-CDs-1 with commercial phosphors in appropriate proportions, a high-quality white light-emitting diode (WLED) was successfully fabricated, achieving a high CRI of 97. Moreover, the CCT of this WLED closely approximates natural light, offering significant advantages for practical lighting applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of fluorescent properties of R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis steps for R-CDs are illustrated in Scheme 1 . R-CDs-1 was prepared using a 1:1 M ratio of pyromellitic acid to 1,8-diaminonaphthalene. Similar synthesis procedures were applied to the other four groups of samples, varying only the molar ratios of pyromellitic acid to 1,8-diaminonaphthalene (while keeping the total molar amount constant). Under the same reaction conditions, this yielded R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-0.5, and R-CDs-0.25. Due to its longer emission wavelength compared to the other groups, R-CDs-1 was selected for further application studies.

Five groups of R-CDs synthesized were dispersed in ethanol solutions for optical characterization to investigate their optical properties. Details of the fluorescence characterization of R-CDs can be found in Fig. 1 and Table 1 . From R-CDs-4 to R-CDs-0.25, the fluorescence exhibits a phenomenon of initial redshift followed by a blue shift. R-CDs-1 exhibits an optimal emission wavelength of 671 nm when excited at a wavelength of 564 nm, which is the longest wavelength among the five sets of R-CDs. The excitation-dependence was further explored, showing an initial increase followed by a decrease in emission intensity with increasing excitation wavelength for R-CDs-4 to R-CDs-0.25, with emission peak positions remaining unchanged, indicating non-excited-state wavelength dependence (Fig. 1a1-e1). Fluorescence lifetime measurements of the five groups of R-CDs (Fig. 1a2-e2) revealed lifetimes of 1.35 ns for R-CDs-4 and R-CDs-2, 1.14 ns for R-CDs-1, 1.48 ns for R-CDs-0.5, and 5.2 ns for R-CDs-0.25. Additionally, absolute QY were tested (Table 1), showing values of 12.9 %, 15.5 %, 25.1 %, 17.9 %, and 10.8 % for R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5, and R-CDs-0.25, respectively. R-CDs-1 exhibited the longest emission wavelength and relatively high QY, while R-CDs-0.25 showed the shortest emission wavelength and longest fluorescence lifetime, likely due to significant overlap between absorption and excitation peaks of R-CDs-0.25, resulting in fewer non-radiative transitions. Additionally, Tauc analysis of the UV–visible spectra for the five sets of R-CDs, employing the equation Eg = 1240/λedge, reveals that the bandgap energies corresponding to these absorption peaks (as shown in Fig. 1a3-e3) exhibit a trend of initial decrease followed by an increase. Based on this analysis, an energy level diagram was plotted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The effect of R-CDs structure on fluorescence performance
ce:section.ce:para: Due to the varied fluorescent properties of the five groups of carbon dots prepared, the factors potentially influencing their luminescent behavior were explored next. Basic morphology characterization was first conducted using TEM, Fig. 2 a–e reveal that all five groups of samples, R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5 and R-CDs-0.25, are uniformly dispersed and exhibit good dispersibility, with average particle sizes measured at 1.72 nm, 2.33 nm, 2.42 nm, 1.81 nm, and 1.51 nm, respectively. Overall, as the proportion of phthalic acid in the precursor decreases, the average size of the carbon dots first increases and then decreases, with R-CDs-1 exhibiting the largest size. Based on this trend, the corresponding size distribution was simulated and depicted, as shown in Fig. 2a3-e3. HR-TEM images in Fig. 3 a2-e2 show distinct lattice fringes for R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5 and R-CDs-0.25, while no lattice structure was observed for R-CDs-4. The lattice fringe spacing for R-CDs-2 is approximately 0.331 nm, corresponding to the graphite (002) plane; for R-CDs-1, it is about 0.215 nm, corresponding to the graphite (100) plane; for R-CDs-0.5, about 0.206 nm, corresponding to the graphite (101) plane; and for R-CDs-0.25, about 0.216 nm, also corresponding to the graphite (100) plane. This indicates that in the R-CDs-4 system, an excess of phthalic acid prevents the formation of well-defined conjugated structures during carbonization, resulting in lower conjugation degrees and indistinct lattice fringes in HR-TEM observations. Conversely, with an increased proportion of naphthalenediamine in the system, enhanced carbonization leads to more pronounced conjugated structures within the carbon cores, thereby demonstrating clear lattice fringes under HR-TEM.

The functional groups were characterized using XPS. According to the full spectra in Fig. 3a–e, all five sets of samples contain the elements C, N, and O. However, their relative proportions vary slightly. In R-CDs-1, the percentages of C 1 s (285 eV), N 1 s (400 eV), and O 1 s (532 eV) peaks are 82.2 %, 5.9 %, and 11.9 %, respectively. Detailed data for the other samples are provided in Table S1. Overall, increasing the proportion of naphthalenediamine in the precursor mix leads to higher C content and lower O content. Notably, R-CDs-1 has the highest proportion of N element (Table S1).

Elemental forms in the samples were characterized by high-resolution XPS spectra as shown in Fig. 3a1-e3, using peak fitting. Taking R-CDs-1 as an example, carbon primarily exists in forms of CC/C C (284.6 eV), C N (285.95 eV), and CO/C O (288.5 eV). Nitrogen is mainly present as pyridinic nitrogen (398.4 eV), graphitic nitrogen (398.8 eV), and amino nitrogen (400.15 eV). Oxygen exists predominantly as C O (531.5 eV) and CO (533.05 eV). Similar forms of C, N, and O elements were observed in the other four sets of samples, detailed in Table S2, showing varying proportions of chemical bonds among different samples. In R-CDs, carbon is primarily in the form of CC/CC, indicating these carbon dots are internally structured with extensive conjugation, while their surfaces exhibit surface functional groups such as amide bonds. Additionally, FT-IR spectroscopy reveals the formation of amide bonds at 1640 cm−1 in R-CDs-1, indicating a reaction between pyromellitic acid and 1,8-diaminonaphthalene [12,13]. The formation of R-CDs in this study aligns with the previously reported mechanisms of carbon dot synthesis. The precursors first undergo amidation and condensation reactions, followed by polymerization to form larger fragments, which eventually crosslink and carbonize to yield the final R-CDs [14–16]. Furthermore, the types of surface functional groups among the five sample groups showed no significant differences, consistent with XPS results (as shown in Fig. S1). Based on the conclusions from the aforementioned characterization tests, we simulated and constructed the basic structures of the five sample sets, as shown in Fig. 3a4-e4.

To further elucidate the influence of carbon dot structure on their fluorescence properties, simulations and theoretical calculations were conducted using DFT and Time-Dependent (TD) DFT. These calculations incorporated five sets of samples, assessing the elemental and chemical bond proportions, as well as their respective sizes. Optimization of ground-state structures utilized the RB3LYP theoretical level and the 6-311 G(d) basis set.

Firstly, it should be noted that simplified pyrene-based models (∼1 nm) can be used to reveal relative trends and aid in explaining the photoluminescent mechanisms of larger-sized CDs [1]. Therefore, a typical model based on pyrene [17] was chosen to simulate five groups of carbon dots, where the dot sizes vary initially increase and then decrease with changing proportions, as shown in Fig. 4 a. This model features sp2 domains with multiple fused aromatic rings, which are further functionalized with varying numbers of amide and carboxylic acid groups. Additionally, to ensure that the simulated data more closely aligns with real-world conditions, we compared the elemental composition of the R-CDs structures obtained through DFT simulations with the actual XPS results of R-CDs. As shown in Table S3, there is a strong agreement between the two, and the DFT simulations effectively reproduce the trends in elemental composition across the five sets of R-CDs samples. Simulation calculations were conducted for five representative models labeled R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5 and R-CDs-0.25. As depicted in Fig. 4b, the LUMO and HOMO energy levels of these five models were computed using Equation: E gap = E(LUMO) − E(HOMO), the E gap values calculated for R-CDs-4, R-CDs-2, R-CDs-1, R-CDs-0.5 and R-CDs-0.25 are 2.55 eV, 3.51 eV, 3.37 eV, 3.49 eV, and 3.84 eV, respectively. It is evident that with increasing conjugation and amide content, E gap decreases significantly. This correlates with the redshift in emission wavelength observed in the experimental analysis discussed earlier, further substantiating the photoluminescent mechanism of R-CDs.

In summary, the fluorescence mechanisms of these five groups of carbon dots can be explained by synergistic interactions between the carbon core and surface states. For R-CDs-4 and R-CDs-0.25, R-CDs-4 exhibits lower carbonization extent (Fig. 2a2), while R-CDs-0.25, despite having the highest proportion of CC/CC bonds, emits at shorter wavelengths due to its smallest size and thus smaller sp2 conjugated structure domains [18]. In the cases of R-CDs-2 and R-CDs-0.5, increasing surface amide content leads to an increase in lone pair electrons on surface groups and enhanced π-conjugation between carbon core electrons, resulting in a decreased E gap and red-shifted emission wavelength [1]. Additionally, for R-CDs-1 with the largest sp2 conjugated domain, the extensive π-electron delocalization results in the smallest E gap and the longest emission wavelength. Therefore, the increase in sp2 conjugated domains and surface amide content in R-CDs leads to a smaller E gap, which is the primary factor causing red-shifted fluorescence emission.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solid-state luminescence and WLEDs based on R-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Due to R-CDs-1 exhibiting the longest emission wavelength and favorable fluorescence properties, its potential application in solid-state lighting was further explored. A fluorescent solid epoxy resin petal was prepared by incorporating R-CDs-1, as depicted in Fig. S2. Under natural light, the petal model appears orange, while under UV irradiation, it emits red fluorescence. This effect is attributed to the introduction of epoxy resin, which hinders the aggregation of R-CDs-1, suppresses π–π stacking, and thus prevents fluorescence quenching [19]. Furthermore, our observations demonstrate that R-CDs-1 exhibit excellent photostability both in solution and within solid epoxy resin petals (Fig. S3). Additionally, R-CDs-1 display remarkable thermal stability (Fig. S4).

Considering the solid-state luminescence potential of R-CDs-1, it was incorporated as a phosphor with a UV chip to fabricate a monochromatic LED. As shown in Fig. S5, the LED exhibits CIE coordinates of (0.61, 0.38) and a CCT of 1329 K, demonstrating emission centered around 650 nm. Further efforts were made to construct a WLED using R-CDs-1. R-CDs-1 was mixed with commercial phosphors: blue phosphor BAM (BaMgAl10O17:Eu2+) and green phosphor （Ba, Sr）2SiO4:Eu2+. These mixed phosphors were uniformly dispersed in epoxy resin silicone resin and applied onto a UV-LED chip centered around an emission wavelength of 365 nm. As shown in Fig. 5 a, b, the prepared WLED emits bright white light at a driving current of 50 mA, with CIE coordinates of (0.36, 0.36), a high CRI of 97, and a CCT of 4664 K. The electroluminescence spectrum of the WLED covers a continuous broadband across the entire visible range from 350 to 850 nm, as depicted in Fig. 5c. The emission peak at 365 nm originates from the UV-LED chip, while the peaks at 454 nm and 530 nm derive from the blue and green phosphors, respectively. The emission peak at 650 nm is attributed to R-CDs-1. In WLEDs based on trichromatic emission, adding red light often enhances their illumination performance significantly, crucially improving their CRI. WLEDs fabricated with R-CDs-1 demonstrate superior illumination capabilities, owing to their significant red light emission (48.5 %). The emission spectrum comprises 24.2 % blue, 27.3 % green, and 48.5 % red components. The CRI assesses a light source's ability to accurately reproduce object colors, with natural light (like sunlight) having a CRI of 100 as the benchmark. To visually evaluate the color rendering of WLEDs, images of colored strips under sunlight and WLED illumination were compared. As depicted in Fig. 5d, e, the images under WLED lighting closely resemble those under sunlight, indicating excellent color fidelity in WLEDs. In addition, the stability of the device under different environmental conditions is critical for its practical applications. Therefore, we conducted relevant tests, as shown in Fig. S6. Under varying current conditions, the WLED exhibits nearly unchanged EL spectra, and the CIE coordinates show no significant variation. Notably, the CRI remains consistently high, ranging between 96.4 and 97, indicating excellent electroluminescent stability of the WLED.

Comparative analysis reveals that WLEDs fabricated using R-CDs-1 exhibit superior color rendering performance compared to the majority of previously reported WLEDs, reaching values close to the highest reported in current literature, as shown in Table 2 . It is noteworthy that CCT reflects the color characteristics of light emitted by a source, perceptually categorized as warm or cool. Lower CCT values indicate warmer light, appearing orange-yellow or yellow, whereas higher CCT values indicate cooler light, appearing blue-white or white [20]. The WLEDs prepared in this study have a CCT of 4664 K. Compared to WLEDs prepared with CDs@MCC and WCDs, those fabricated with R-CDs-1 have a CCT closer to natural white light, offering more neutral illumination suitable for maintaining indoor clarity and color fidelity, thereby creating comfortable environments for work and study. Consequently, WLEDs prepared with R-CDs-1 exhibit promising application potential in settings such as offices and schools that require clear and comfortable lighting.

In summary, this study prepared near-infrared carbon dots, termed R-CDs-1, emitting at 671 nm using a straightforward solvent-thermal method. The morphology and structure of the samples were characterized, and through simulation calculations, the influence of the carbon dots' structure on their fluorescence was investigated. In R-CDs-1, the photoluminescence is predominantly influenced by the carbon core state, with increased amide content and conjugation enhancing the redshift of the emission spectrum. Theoretical calculations confirming reduced bandgap further supported this redshift in fluorescence emission. Due to R-CDs-1′s long-wavelength emission, non-excitation dependency, and excellent fluorescence stability, it holds potential for fabricating white light-emitting diodes (WLEDs) with tri-primary colors. Mixing R-CDs-1 with commercial phosphors in specific ratios successfully produced high-quality WLEDs with a CRI of 97, the highest reported to date. Moreover, this WLED exhibits a color temperature closer to natural light, offering superior advantages for practical lighting applications.

Fanghao Wang: Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Kexin Wang: Visualization, Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization. Ruifang Guan: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Resources, Investigation, Data curation, Conceptualization. Hao Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Methodology, Investigation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work did not receive any financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2025.137416.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
